attributes.stem:
A 42-year-old woman presents with a two-year history of increasing generalised weakness and weight gain of about 15 kg. She initially ignored these symptoms but recently has been experiencing back pain, feels melancholic, and now seeks advice. Her history includes mild hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed five years ago.

On examination, her BMI is 33, blood pressure is 140/85 mmHg, and pulse is 70/min. There is central obesity, with fat deposition on the trunk and the back of her neck. She has facial hair, wide purple abdominal striae, and a dorsocervical fat pad.

A plain X-ray of her spine shows a compressed T11 fracture.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would be the most appropriate initial investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient likely has Cushing's syndrome given the constellation of weight gain, striae (without prior history of pregnancy), new-onset diabetes, hypertension and vertebral compression fracture indicative of osteoporosis. The diagnosis of Cushing's syndrome requires that at least two of the three first-line tests are positive. These include:

  • 24-hour urinary-free cortisol level

  • low-dose dexamethasone suppression test

  • midnight salivary cortisol level

A 24-hour urinary-free cortisol (Free in this instance merely means not protein-bound) is a screening test for Cushing's syndrome. Patients with Cushing's syndrome have abnormally high levels of cortisol, which can be detected in a 24-hour urine collection. This test is useful in differentiating between Cushing's syndrome and other conditions that can cause similar symptoms. Given the patient's clinical presentation and findings, a 24-hour urine-free cortisol would be the most appropriate initial test.

Serum adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) is not used for screening and is only helpful in determining the aetiology once the diagnosis of Cushing syndrome has been made. It helps establish ACTH-dependent from ACTH-independent aetiologies, narrowing the differential diagnosis and subsequent evaluation. Serum ACTH is a hormone that stimulates the production and release of cortisol from the adrenal glands. In patients with Cushing's syndrome, there are two main types: ACTH-dependent (usually caused by a pituitary tumour) and ACTH-independent (usually caused by an adrenal tumour). Measuring serum ACTH levels can help differentiate between these two types.

An MRI brain can be useful in evaluating patients with suspected pituitary tumours causing Cushing's syndrome. But this test is premature at this point, as the diagnosis of Cushing syndrome has not yet been confirmed.

A CT abdomen can be useful in evaluating patients with suspected adrenal tumours causing Cushing's syndrome. But this test is premature at this point, as the diagnosis of Cushing syndrome has not yet been confirmed.

Corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) stimulation test is only used once hypercortisolism has been established and ACTH is elevated, as it helps distinguish Cushing disease versus ectopic ACTH production. The CRH stimulation test is a diagnostic test used to differentiate between ACTH-dependent and ACTH-independent causes of Cushing's syndrome. In this test, CRH is administered, and the response of cortisol and ACTH levels are measured.

Both MRI pituitary and CT adrenals can miss some smaller but still clinically significant lesions, thus limiting their sensitivity and requiring confirmation with functional testing.
learningPoints: The 24-hour urine free cortisol test can detect endogenous overproduction of cortisol.
This test is useful in differentiating between Cushing's syndrome and other conditions that can cause similar symptoms.
references: Chaudhry HS, Singh G. Cushing Syndrome. [Updated 2023 Jan 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-.

Nieman LK, Biller BM, Findling JW, et al. The Diagnosis of Cushing’s Syndrome: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2008;93(5):1526-1540. doi: 10.1210/jc.2008-0125.
